title: SEGMENT 9 — Splicing and Rev: getting the right RNA to the right place

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — Splicing and Rev: getting the right RNA to the right place
So Pol II has finished its job.
We have a 9-kilobase capped, polyadenylated, unspliced HIV-1 primary transcript sitting in the nucleus.
From this single transcript, the virus needs to make at least three categories of mRNA — and also keep some of the transcript intact to use as the genome of new virions.
This is non-trivial.
Let me lay out the categories.
Unspliced RNA — the full 9 kilobase transcript.
Used as the mRNA for Gag and Gag-Pol.
Also packaged as the genome.
Singly spliced RNAs — the genomic 5' end is joined to one downstream splice acceptor, skipping over gag and pol.
These encode Env, and the accessory proteins Vif, Vpr, and Vpu.
Multiply spliced RNAs — both splicing events occur, producing very short mRNAs that encode Tat, Rev, and Nef.

Splicing in eukaryotes is normally tightly coupled to nuclear export — fully spliced mRNAs get exported, unspliced and partially spliced transcripts are retained in the nucleus and eventually degraded.
This is a quality-control mechanism to prevent intron-containing transcripts from being translated.
But HIV needs to get its unspliced and partially spliced transcripts out of the nucleus, intact, to make Gag, Gag-Pol, and Env, and to package the genome.
The solution is a second viral regulatory protein called Rev.
Rev is a small protein.
It's encoded by a multiply spliced mRNA, so the first viral proteins to be made early in infection are Tat, Rev, and Nef.
Rev shuttles into the nucleus.
It binds, with high specificity, a structured RNA element called the Rev Response Element, or RRE, which sits inside the env coding region.
Because RRE is inside env, it is present in the unspliced and singly spliced transcripts — the ones the cell wants to retain — but it is spliced out of the multiply spliced transcripts.

Once Rev binds RRE, it oligomerizes — typically about eight Rev molecules pile onto a single RRE — and recruits a host export factor called CRM1 (also called exportin-1), which is the same factor cells use to export certain proteins and snoRNAs.
CRM1 binds Rev, the whole complex moves through the nuclear pore, and the unspliced or singly spliced RNA goes with it.
So early in infection, before Rev levels build up, only the multiply spliced transcripts get out — making more Tat, more Rev, more Nef.
Once Rev has accumulated, it starts pulling out the longer RNAs, and gag, pol, env, vif, vpr, and vpu protein production begins.
This temporal switch from "early" to "late" gene expression is set by Rev concentration.
There's a famous drug, selinexor, used in multiple myeloma, that inhibits CRM1.
It has been studied for HIV applications but is too toxic for that use.
The principle is interesting nonetheless — block CRM1, you block Rev, you collapse the late phase of HIV gene expression.
A postgraduate footnote: simpler retroviruses don't have Rev.

Murine leukemia virus, for instance, exports its unspliced RNA using a different cis-acting element called a constitutive transport element, CTE, that recruits a different host export pathway involving NXF1/TAP.
So the regulatory architecture is varied, but the problem is universal — retroviruses must export their long, intron-containing RNAs against host quality control.
OK.
We have all our mRNAs in the cytoplasm.
We have full-length genomic RNA waiting to be packaged.
Now translation.
